coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots: Synthesis via hydrothermal processing, doping strategies, integration with photocatalysts, and their application in photocatalytic hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a quasi-spherical carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted great attention in photocatalysis due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties, such as tunable fluorescence emission, up-conversion property, and the ability to accelerate charge separation. Significant efforts have been made to synthesize, dope, and surface-functionalize CQDs to tune their photo-physical/chemical properties. Hydrothermal (HT) processes provide a clean, cost-effective, and efficient synthesis method to prepare CQDs and their derivatives, through hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization reactions. This review summarizes the synthesis of HT-CQDs using various precursors, followed by evaluating the CQDs doping and heterostructure formation strategies based on their fundamental characteristics (e.g., morphology, crystallinity, absorption ability, and photoluminescent properties). Doped CQDs exhibit enhanced optical properties, improved charge transfer efficiency, better electron mobility, and increased photocatalytic activity through introducing new surface states and active sites. Decorating traditional photocatalysts with CQDs improves light absorption and charge separation, significantly boosting the overall photocatalytic activity. The performance of CQD-based photocatalysts in hydrogen (H2) evolution is systematically evaluated as well. CQDs enhance photocatalytic H2 generation by acting as photosensitizers and/or electron mediators, accelerating the separation of e−-h+ pairs and reducing recombination rates. This review highlights the significant advancement in CQDs synthesis, and doping and decorating strategies, showcasing their pivotal role in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of H2 production. However, limited understanding of mechanisms, precise control over doping and surface functionalization, and scalability of synthesis methods remain key challenges for CQD-based photocatalysts. In the future, integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) tools with advanced characterization techniques might help the development of CQD-based photocatalysts, enabling precise property tuning and scalable synthesis approaches.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a quasi-spherical carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted great attention in photocatalysis due to their unique optical and electrochemical properties, such as tunable fluorescence emission, up-conversion property, and the ability to accelerate charge separation. Significant efforts have been made to synthesize, dope, and surface-functionalize CQDs to tune their photo-physical/chemical properties. Hydrothermal (HT) processes provide a clean, cost-effective, and efficient synthesis method to prepare CQDs and their derivatives, through hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization reactions. This review summarizes the synthesis of HT-CQDs using various precursors, followed by evaluating the CQDs doping and heterostructure formation strategies based on their fundamental characteristics (e.g., morphology, crystallinity, absorption ability, and photoluminescent properties). Doped CQDs exhibit enhanced optical properties, improved charge transfer efficiency, better electron mobility, and increased photocatalytic activity through introducing new surface states and active sites. Decorating traditional photocatalysts with CQDs improves light absorption and charge separation, significantly boosting the overall photocatalytic activity. The performance of CQD-based photocatalysts in hydrogen (H2) evolution is systematically evaluated as well. CQDs enhance photocatalytic H2 generation by acting as photosensitizers and/or electron mediators, accelerating the separation of e−-h+ pairs and reducing recombination rates. This review highlights the significant advancement in CQDs synthesis, and doping and decorating strategies, showcasing their pivotal role in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of H2 production. However, limited understanding of mechanisms, precise control over doping and surface functionalization, and scalability of synthesis methods remain key challenges for CQD-based photocatalysts. In the future, integrating artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) tools with advanced characterization techniques might help the development of CQD-based photocatalysts, enabling precise property tuning and scalable synthesis approaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution and energy shortage have become critical factors hindering the sustainable development of human society [1]. The exploration of renewable energy sources and innovative clean technologies are of paramount importance to address those two issues [2]. Among various renewable energy, hydrogen (H₂) has emerged as a clean fuel with immense potential due to its high energy density, abundance, and zero‑carbon emissions upon combustion [3]. However, most H2 is produced via steam methane reforming (SMR), a process reliant on fossil fuels, contributing to significant GHG emissions, thus is classified as ‘gray H2’ [4]. Photocatalysis emerges as a promising clean technology for solar-driven ‘green H2’ production, offering a sustainable pathway to alleviate energy scarcity and environmental degradation [5,6,7].

Photocatalytic H2 generation harnesses solar energy to split water into H2 and O2 by using photocatalyst, typically semiconductors. It primarily depends on the efficiency of photoreaction when semiconductors are exposed to light irradiation. In this process, electrons (e−) are excited from valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving behind holes (h+) in the VB. These photo-induced charge carriers are termed as the e−-h+ pairs. Once e−-h+ pairs are separated, these can be used to drive redox processes on the surface of the catalyst such as degradation of pollutants (through oxidation reactions near VB) and H2 production (via reduction reaction near the CB) (Fig. 1 ). By combining these processes, simultaneous pollutant degradation and energy production can occur synergistically [8]. Therefore, effective photocatalysts applied in H2 generation are required to have high light absorption capacity, efficient separation of e−-h+ pairs, high stability, and low cost. Techno-economic analyses indicate that advancements in photocatalyst development and its scalability are critical for reducing production costs and enhancing STH efficiency, making green H2 competitive with conventional methods [9].

In recent years, different types of photocatalysts have gained wide attention and been explored for their photocatalytic activities. However, most of them still suffer from limited visible light utilization, high agglomeration rates, low surface areas, and high recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs [10]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an quasi-spherical carbon-based nanomaterial, was first discovered in the process of purification and separation of carbon nanotubes in 2004 [11], and quickly stand out due to their favorable photocatalytic properties, including exceptional optical and electronic characteristics pertinent to light-induced electron transfer, multiple emission traps, small size, non-toxicity, and hydrophilic nature [12]. To further enhance the photocatalytic activity of CQDs, doping with non-metal elements (e.g., nitrogen and phosphorus) and metals (e.g. copper) have been explored [13, 14]. CQDs have also been effectively incorporated into traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, such as TiO2, g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, ZnO, and others [15,16,17,18,19]. This integration has the potential to address several limitations of these semiconductors in H2 generation by (i) adsorbing radiation from a broader spectral range of light through the up-conversion property of CQDs [20,21]; (ii) acting as an electron collector to enhance separation efficiency of e−-h+ pair. [22]; (iii) providing more active sites for redox reactions [23,24]; (iv) improving photo stability through the fabrication of special CQDs structures, such as the core-shell configurations [25].

CQDs synthesis methods can be categorized into “top-down” approaches (laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, and chemical oxidation) and “bottom-up” approaches (pyrolytic carbonization, hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted synthesis) [26]. Among them, hydrothermal (HT) processes has attracted great interest due to its advantages of cleanliness, cost-effectiveness, and efficiency [27]. Research efforts have been dedicated to studying CQD-based photocatalysts synthesized through HT processes. However, there is only one relevant review paper available (Nazibudin et al. [27], however, it does not include the vital information pertaining to the doping of HT-CQDs and the integration of HT-CQDs into traditional photocatalysts.

This review aims to advance the understanding of CQDs-based photocatalyst, focusing on doping elements into CQDs and incorporating CQDs into traditional photocatalysts. First the synthesis of CQDs by HT using different types of feedstocks is briefly discussed, followed by a detailed examination of doping. This section distinguishes between metal doping, non-metal doping, and co-doping and highlights how different doping strategies can modify the physiochemical properties of CQDs and enhance their photocatalytic performance. The effects of decorating traditional photocatalyst with CQDs on absorption capacity, fluorescence properties, photocurrent, photostability properties is comprehensively discussed. In addition, the performance of CQDs-based photocatalysts in H2 production has been thoroughly evaluated.

HT processes provide a green route to produce carbon-based nanomaterial from various precursors [27], including chemical compounds, biomass, and other carbon sources. HT-CQDs are fabricated in sub-critical liquid water, involving a carbonization process to convert carbon precursors into CQDs at a moderate temperature (180–250 °C) and self-built pressures (2–6MPa) [28,29], as illustrated in CQD synthesis by HT (Fig. 2 ) consists of four steps: hydrolysis, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [30]. Initially, large organic molecules are hydrolyzed into smaller molecular fragments in an aqueous environment, where high temperature and pressure break down chemical bonds. These fragments then undergo dehydration, losing water molecules and becoming more reactive, followed by polymerization, during which they combine to form the complex, large polymeric structure. The resulting polymers are subjected to carbonization, transforming them into condensed carbon clusters composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. As the reaction advances, these clusters nucleate and coalesce into the discrete, nanoscopic crystalline structures known as CQDs.

The CQDs synthesized via the HT process are primarily affected by three factors: temperature, reaction time, feedstock ratio, and type of precursors. The HT temperature directly affects the rate of hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization reactions. Generally, a temperature range of 180 °C to 300 °C is required for the synthesis of CQDs via the HT process [27]. Theoretically, higher temperatures can enhance reaction rates, leading to a fast formation of carbon clusters and CQDs. However, at excessively high temperatures (over 300 °C), the carbon source might be overcarbonizated, destroying the surface structure of CQDs [27]. Meanwhile, the duration of HT treatment determines the extent to which the reactions proceed. Research indicates that the reaction time for CQD synthesis falls within the range of 4 to 12 h. Longer reaction times can promote the growth of larger CQDs and affect their crystallinity by more deeply carbonizing them [31]. The chemical and structural properties of CQDs synthesized via the HT process are highly of feedstock dependence. The molecular complexity and functional group composition of the carbon source directly influence the hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization pathways, further impacting the quantum yield (QY), particle size, and fluorescent properties of CQDs [26]. The purity and reactivity of the precursors determine the efficiency of conversion and the extent of purification required after synthesis. Feedstock ratio is also significantly affects the quality and properties of the CQDs. A higher feedstock ratio can increase the carbon source availability, promoting carbonization and enhancing CQD yield. However, excessively high ratios may lead to aggregation and uneven carbonization, resulting in CQDs with poor surface properties or reduced optical performance.

CQDs are defined as nanostructured carbon materials that commonly have a quasi-spherical structure and a super-small size ranging from 2 to 10 nm [32]. CQDs exhibit high size dependence, primarily due to the quantum confinement effect. This effect occurs when carbon nanoparticles are smaller than the exciton Bohr radius of carbon matrix material (2–5 nm), causing the movement of charge carriers to be confined within a very small space. This confinement results in the transition of energy levels from quasi-continuous to discrete states, which fundamentally shapes the electronic and optical properties of CQDs [33]. CQDs have a carbon basic skeleton composed of sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon structure, forming a special conjugated π-structure [10]. They are attached to numerous functional groups, such as carbonyl (C=O) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the surface [10]. The synergetic interaction between the carbon structure and the attached functional groups directly impacts the optical properties of CQDs. The crystallinity of CQDs ranges from amorphous to nanocrystalline, determined by carbonization degree, with an interlayer distance of around 0.34 nm, which is close to that of graphitic carbon [34].

The sp2 or sp3- conjugated carbon structure of CQDs results in broad absorption capacity in UV–vis spectral range [10]. Typically, CQDs present one absorption peak in UV region (240–280 nm), which is assigned to π-π transitions of CC and CC bonds in aromatic π systems. In some cases, due to doping with atoms (e.g., N, S and P) or oxidation occurrence, CQDs could exhibit second absorption peaks (300–450 nm), the latter of which corresponds to n-π transition of CO group [10]. The position and presence of additional absorption peaks are tailored by functional groups through introducing new electronic states or surface passivation. Therefore, to achieve optimal light absorption properties, appropriate surface modification of CQDs, such as surface passivation, is essential.

Fluorescence emission properties of CQDs are primarily attributed to main bandgap transition of conjugated π-structure, along with the synergistic effects of radiative and non-radiative recombination processes [35]. Both radiative and non-radiative recombination occurs when the electrons return to the ground state and recombine with holes. Radiative recombination occurs when electrons and holes recombine at shallow defect states, resulting in photon emission, which is fluorescence behaviors [36]. It is generally believed that the size of carbogenic core and degree of graphitization are crucial factors to affect the radiative recombination [37]. The wavelength of the emitted light is proportional to the size of CQDs. The small CQDs, size lower than 1.5 nm, emit ultraviolet light around 350 nm. The medium sized CQDs (1.5 nm to 3 nm) emit visible light ranging 400 nm to 700 nm, and the large CQDs (3 nm to 10 nm) show near-infrared emission with wavelength ≥ 800 nm [10]. Conversely, non-radiative recombination is influenced by the presence of deep defects which trap π electrons or holes for a long time, preventing them radiatively recombine with each other. Instead, the π electrons and holes undergo vibrational relaxation, allowing them to recombine non-radiatively to release energy as heat rather than emitting light [38]. Non-radiative recombination pathway diminishes the availability of charge carriers, negatively impacting photocatalytic activity. Thus, precise control over defect formation is crucial for tuning the fluorescence properties and enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CQDs.

Up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), the most attractive feature of CQDs in photocatalysis, was first observed by Cao et al. [39]. The UCPL phenomenon in CQDs is primarily attributed to anti-Stokes emission via multi-photon absorption. In this mechanism, two or more low energy photons are absorbed nearly simultaneously, and their combined energy excited an electron to a higher energy state. The excited electron subsequently relaxes to the ground state, emitting a photon of higher energy (shorter wavelength) than the incident photons [40]. In the case of doped CQDs, UCPL is often associated with energy transfer up conversion (ETU) processes. ETU involves stepwise energy transfer between nearby dopant atoms or nanostructures, allowing one site donates energy to another, cumulatively promoting an electron to a higher energy state. The defect states and trap levels introduced by doping serve as intermediate energy levels, enabling sequential photon absorption under near-UV or visible light, facilitating the generation of anti-Stokes emission [41]. More details about the optical properties of CQDs are discussed in Sections 4, 5, 6 and 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield and apparent quantum efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is an important index to characterize the photon emission efficiency of CQDs [42]. It assesses the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons. A larger QY suggests a stronger fluorescence of CQDs. The QY is obtained by a comparative method, and is calculated as the following equation [43].
Q
Y S
%
= Q
Y R
×
I S
I R
×
OD R
OD S
×
η S 2
η R 2
where S refers to the sample, and R refers to the reference quinine sulphate. I is the photoluminescence area, OD is the absorbance value, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) is a measure used in photocatalysis to quantify the efficiency with which a photocatalyst converts absorbed photons into a chemical reaction, such as H2 generation in photocatalytic water splitting. AQE is the ratio of the number of molecules formed to the number of photons absorbed by the photocatalyst, as shown in the following equation.
AQE
%
=
n × Rate of
H 2
generation
mol s
Rate of incident photons
photons s
× 100
where n refers to the number of electrons transferred for the evolution of one molecule of H2 (n = 2 for H2 evolution reaction).

This section evaluates the performance of various materials used for CQDs synthesis via HT processes, categorized into Section 4.1 (chemical reagents), Section 4.2 (raw biomass), and Section 4.3 (other sources). The performance of CQDs is primarily assessed by their QY, the most important index for CQDs synthesis, and summarized in Table 1 .

Chemical reagents, such as saccharides, amines, and organic acids, are the most commonly used carbon precursors in preparation of CQDs [27], due to their abundant carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Glucose, a typical monosaccharide, is frequently utilized for HT-CQDs synthesis, in which the hydroxyl groups offer numerous reactive sites to achieve efficient hydrolysis reaction. Jing et al. [44] demonstrated that by coupling HT processing of glucose with alkaline-peroxide treatment, a high QY of 22.67 % can be obtained. The study emphasized that glucose oxidation during synthesis resulted in CQDs with abundant carbonyl and carboxyl groups on their surface, as well as conjugated double bonds and quinoid structures (Fig. 3a). Toma et al. [17] fabricated core-shell CQDs from humin using HT processes at 160–200 °C for 4–12 h. The resulting CQDs had a QY of 22.6 %, with plentiful hydroxyl groups. Interestingly, unlike conventional CQDs with one or two absorption peaks, these humin-derived CQDs displayed three peaks at 220 nm, 335 nm, and 354 nm in the UV region (Fig. 3b). The third absorption peak was attributed to an overtone transition of an oxygen-bridged bond (R-O-R') in the sp3 hybrid region. The feasibility of using N-containing polysaccharide for CQDs synthesis has also been illustrated. Wang et al. [45] utilized chitosan to produce N-doped CQDs with a notable QY of 31.8 %. The enhanced QY was attributed to the N content in chitosan, which was incorporated into the N-CQDs as graphite-like structure NC3 (10 %), pyridinic-like N (67 %), and NH bonds of NH2 groups (23 %).

Organic acids (e.g., citric acid) characterized by the presence of one or more carboxylic groups, are usually combined with amine-based chemicals (e.g., ethylenediamine) for CQDs synthesis. Chahal et al. [49] explored the synthesis of CQDs using citric acid, phenylalanine, and their mixtures at seven different ratios (Fig. 3c). The results showed that increasing the phenylalanine content in the mixture led to a significant improvement in QY, reaching a maximum of 65 %. In contrast, the QY for citric acid and phenylalanine alone remained low at 2 % and 3.4 %, respectively. This highlights the limitations of using organic acids or amines alone and emphasizes the interdependence of these components for achieving high QYs. Hence, to improve CQDs fluorescence properties, organic acids are often combined with nitrogen-rich amines, such as ethylenediamine, which can serve as a good N source. Wu et al. [46] used ethylenediamine as a N source to assist citric acid in generation of N-doped CQDs, and as-prepared N-CQDs reached a remarkable QY of 97.4 %. In general, saccharides, amines, and organic acids represent the primary precursors for CQDs synthesis via HT processes. Saccharides (e.g., glucose and chitosan) can independently function as effective carbon sources, while the combination of organic acids and amines proves crucial for achieving high QYs.

Biomass waste, ranging from agricultural residues to industrial by-products, offers a rich source of carbon and heteroatoms necessary for the fabrication of high-quality CQDs. It can be broadly categorized into lignocellulosic and non-lignocellulosic based wastes. Lignocellulosic wastes, composed primarily of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, are particularly valuable as pivotal feedstocks for CQDs synthesis due to the high carbon content [71]. The interaction between hemicellulose and lignin was claimed to positively influence the synthesis efficiency and fluorescence properties of CQDs [50, 43] synthesized CQDs from rice husk using HT processes, and the maximum QY reached 20.80 %. Rice husk contains cellulose (31.12 %), hemicellulose (22.48 %), and lignin (22.34 %), mineral ash (13.87 %), and extractives (2.33 %). The author attributed that the high QY to the synergistic effect between the comparable amount of hemicellulose and lignin. Zhao et al. [51] reported that CQDs derived from hemicellulose had the lowest QY at 2.36 %, CQDs derived from lignin had a QY of 5.48 %, while the QY significantly increased to 13.52 % of raw lignocellulosic feedstock based-CQDs, unfortunately, the reason/mechanism for the higher QY from raw feedstock was not provided. The CQDs formation pathways are extremely complicated when raw biomass is utilized as feedstock, more research efforts on utilizing biomass model components to elucidate the CQDs formation pathways are recommended.

In addition to the woody biomass, biomass containing abundant N has been utilized to synthesize HT-CQDs. In a study of Najjar et al. [54], CQDs sourced from Cordia myxa L achieved a QY of 14 %. The SEM-EDX analysis confirmed the presence of N elements in CQDs, while FTIR detected CN band, both of which contributed to the excellent fluorescence properties of CQDs. Similarly, Smrithi et al. [72] employed HT processes to synthesize CQDs from Cucurbita pepo with a QY of 12.19 %. The author stated that the N-containing surface functional groups were essential for the desirable fluorescence characteristic of as-prepared CQDs. Shahshahanipour et al. [56] synthesized CQDs from Lawsonia Inermis (Henna plant) with super-high QY of 89 % and attributed their excellent fluorescence properties to the abundant of multifunctional surface groups, including -COOH, -OH, -NH2. These findings were in well agreement with the results discussed in Section 4.1.

Compared to chemical precursors, CQDs synthesized from biomass waste offer significant environmental benefits by contributing in waste management efforts. Prasannan and Imae [66] used orange waste peels to prepare CQDs with a high QY of 36 %. Han et al. [58] utilized a mixture of banana peels, orange peels, and dragon fruit peels, to synthesize CQDs, achieving a QY of 35.37 %. In a study of Crista et al. [67], CQDs was synthesized using three types of spent coffee ground (SCG). Although the QY of the SCG-CQDs ranged from 4.3 % to 5.8 %, the author revealed that the fabrication of CQDs from SCG was more environmentally beneficial and sustainable compared to chemical precursors, as demonstrated by a life cycle assessment (Fig. 4 ab).

In addition to chemicals and biomass wastes, a wide range of other substrates can also be utilized to synthesize CQDs through HT processes. For instance, gelatin was used to synthesize CQDs via HT processes at 200 °C for 4 h, achieving a QY of 2.49 % [68]. These CQDs demonstrated strong potential as an antioxidant in mitigating paraquat-induced neurotoxicity. Similarly, the aqua mesophase pitch, an amphiphilic carbonaceous material, was employed to produce CQDs with a high QY of 27.6 % [69]. In this study, the author investigated the impact of HT conditions, temperatures (120, 150, 180 °C) and reaction time (12, 24, 48 h) on the properties of the synthesized CQDs. The optimal particle size was around 2.8 nm, and the strongest PL intensity was observed in AMP-CQDs at 120 °C for 24 h, attributed to the size confinement effect. Annamalai et al. [70] addressed the waste management of zinc‑carbon dry cell batteries by converting the carbon powder extracted from batteries into CQDs via HT processes, using a 5 % ethylenediamine solution as a catalyst. The resulting CQDs exhibited a QY of 13 % and demonstrated high stability in various environments, including exposure to 200 mM NaCl solution.

In conclusion, CQDs can be synthesized from a wide variety of precursors, including chemical compounds, biomass waste, and other unconventional carbon sources. While individual components like glucose or chitosan can serve as effective carbon sources, the combination of organic acids and nitrogen-rich amines proves to be particularly advantageous for achieving high QYs. In contrast, biomass waste provides a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative to chemical precursors. Lignocellulosic materials, rich in carbon and heteroatoms, not only offer high synthesis efficiency but also contribute to waste management by valorizing agricultural and industrial by-products. The interplay between lignin and hemicellulose in biomass sources has been shown to positively impact CQD properties, though further research is needed to elucidate the mechanisms underlying these interactions. Non-lignocellulosic biomass, particularly N-containing sources, has also yielded CQDs with impressive fluorescence characteristics, further broadening the range of potential feedstocks. Additionally, other substrates such as gelatin, aqua mesophase pitch, and carbon from zinc‑carbon dry cell batteries have demonstrated their viability for CQD synthesis. These materials, particularly industrial wastes, not only offer high QYs but also provide functional versatility and stability in various environmental conditions.

HT synthesis builds the fundamental structure and optical baseline of CQDs, while further performance tuning is often achieved through heteroatom doping, which tailors their electronic properties and surface chemistry. The doping of CQDs with heteroatoms has demonstrated significant potential for modulating their physicochemical and optical properties. This is fundamentally achieved by leveraging orbital overlap and electron exchange capabilities of heteroatoms, which induce electronic transitions, act as electron traps, and promote the generation of an internal electric field [73,74,75]. In detail, one contributing factor is the formation of chemical bonds (e.g., NiS) between the dopant atoms and the semiconductor matrix, which reduces interfacial charge transfer resistance and enhances carrier mobility [74]. Additionally, the dopant-induced surface states can act as electron traps, effectively suppressing the recombination of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and promoting directional electron migration toward reduction sites, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, doping often induces band bending effects, such as Schottky junctions or S-scheme heterostructures, which establish internal electric fields that further facilitate the migration of photogenerated electrons to the co-catalyst surface [76]. The synergistic effects of surface states and internal electric fields contribute to improving spatial charge separation and significantly boost overall photocatalytic efficiency.

Doped-CQDs are commonly prepared by incorporating metal atoms (e.g., iron (Fe), manganese, copper, metalloid (silicon), or non-metal atoms (e.g., boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorous (P)) into the original CQDs structure. Additionally, co-doping, involves doping two or more heteroatoms onto CQDs, has also been explored to further enhance CQDs' properties.

This section aims to evaluate the influence of various dopants, including metals, non-metals, and co-dopants, on the properties of CQDs synthesized via HT processes, including particle size, crystallinity and optical properties.

Efforts to dope metal atoms into CQDs have shown several notable effects, including significantly modifying their surface electronic structure, introducing surface states that act as active centers for charge separation and transfer, and resulting in changes in morphology, enhancement in absorption capacity, induction of new surface states, improvement in conductivity, increase in photostability, and reduction in recombination rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs [77,74]. Varying sizes and structures of doped metal atoms leads to morphological change in metal-doped CQDs. Zhang et al. [78] employed HT processes to produce manganese (Mn)-CQDs with adding o-phenylenediamine and anhydrous manganese at 180 °C for 6 h. The spherical-shaped Mn-CQDs had an average size of 6.5 nm and a high degree of amorphous carbon structure with a crystal layer spacing of 0.47 nm, larger than the 0.34 nm, typically seen in graphite structures. Ren et al. [13] synthesized copper (Cu)-CQDs with a maximum particle size of 9.7 nm, and they observed clear lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.23 nm, consistent with the [100] facet. Iron (Fe)-CQDs prepared by Dang et al. [79] at 180 °C for 10 h were reported to have a smaller size of 1.45 nm, suggesting that the small ionic radius of Fe atom (Fe ≈ 0.645 Å
< Mn ≈ 0.81 Å ) easily matched with carbon atom and synergistically reduced the size of the CQDs under a longer reaction time. Interestingly, Cu atoms had little effect on the morphology of CQDs.

Metal ions, particularly transition metal ions, possess easily detachable electrons and unoccupied orbitals on their surface, which benefits the absorption capacity of CQDs-based photocatalysts and boosts their photocatalytic efficiency [47]. Despite difference in chemical structure among various metal dopants, most metal-doped CQDs exhibit a similar absorption profile, characterized by a strong and broad absorption band ranging from 200 nm to 600 nm [79,80], which is assigned to the π-π transitions of CC and CC bonds in aromatic π systems of CQDs. Compared to original CQDs, most metal atoms doped CQDs enhance absorption capabilities. For example, Wang et al. [81] synthesized Mn-CQDs that exhibited significantly higher absorbance across the entire UV-spectrum compared to non-doped CQDs (Fig. 5a). Ansari et al. [82] synthesized Fe-CQDs, which displayed much higher absorption intensity than CQDs.

In terms of photocatalytic efficiency, doping metal atoms into CQDs can induce additional surface and improve conductivity, which promote the separation of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs and thus enhance photocatalytic efficiency. Xu et al. [85] successfully doped Zinc (Zn) atom into CQDs via HT processes, achieving a maximum QY of 32.3 %, a significantly reduced bandgap of 1.61 eV, and a noticeable decrease in PL intensity. The author attributed the observation to the additional energy levels introduced by Zn doping, which reduced the recombination rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs. This finding aligned with the study of Wang et al. [81], in which the Mn-CQDs exhibited red-shifted and excitation-independence PL behavior. The author attributed this to the metal coordinating characteristics that induced new and stable surface states for the CQDs. Zhang et al. [83] conducted an in-situ synthesis of Cu-CQDs via HT processes and applied them in photocatalytic H2 generation, demonstrating a significant improvement in H2 generation rate. The study confirmed the role of Cu doping in enhancing the conductivity of CQDs through a transient photocurrent measurement (Fig. 5b), which increased the charge transition efficiency and facilitated efficient separation of photo-excited e−-h+ pairs. These agree with the study of Kim et al. ., (2021), that sliver (Ag)-doped CQDs also showed excellent conductivity, enhancing e−-h+ pairs transport and collection.

The effect of metal doping on photostability of CQDs has been explored in several studies. Europium (Eu)-doped CQDs prepared by Liu et al. [6] and germanium (Ge)-CQDs synthesized by Yuan et al. [86] both demonstrated high photostability across varying NaCl concentrations, indicating the potential for metal-doped CQDs to perform well in high ionic-strength environment. Nevertheless, some studies have highlighted the pH sensitivity of metal-doped CQDs. For instance, Ren et al. [13] synthesized Cu-CQDs via HT processes, and reported the pH-dependent behavior, with a slight blue shift in emission peaks under acid conditions and a higher PL intensity in the strong alkaline conditions (Fig. 5 cd). These were aligned with [87]. Therefore, while metal doping can significantly enhance the photostability of CQDs in high ionic-strength environments, it also introduces pH sensitivity, highlighting the need for careful consideration of environmental factors in the application of metal-doped CQDs.

In summary, metal doping of CQDs via HT processes has demonstrated significant enhancement in photocatalytic efficiency, absorption capacity, and photostability, alongside morphological changes depending on the dopant used. The introduction of metal atoms, particularly transition metals, not only alters the electronic structure and conductivity of CQDs but also reduces the recombination rate of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs, thereby improving charge separation and photocatalytic performance. However, the effects of doping can vary with environmental conditions, such as pH sensitivity, which may influence the stability and effectiveness of the doped CQDs. Future research should focus on optimizing the type and amount of dopant to balance enhanced performance with environmental stability, ensuring the broad applicability of metal-doped CQDs in various photocatalytic applications.

Nitrogen (N) doping is one of the most prevalent methods for non-metal atom doping in CQDs, as N readily bonds with C atom, owing to their similar atomic size, structure, and multiple valence electrons [88]. N-doping can alter the particle size of CQDs [89], in which N-CQDs were synthesized from sugarcane bagasse biocharand were amino-functionalized by either ethylenediamine (2.5–10 %) or ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (1–3 %) as N source. The author observed a decrease in both particle size and d-spacing (Fig. 6a) as the amount of dopant increased and attributed this to the edge-termination effect at higher amine concentrations.

N-doping has been proven to significantly enhance the absorption ability of CQDs. Yanyan et al. [91], synthesized N-CQDs using citric acid and linear structure polyethyleneimine as a dopant. They observed that N-doping enhanced CQD absorption in visible light, indicated by a redshift in its absorption band, and noted the appearance of an additional absorption peak at 295 nm. This finding is consistent with the observation in the study by Nugraha et al. [89]. However, Sarkar et al. [92] claimed that only the CQDs synthesized with graphitic N shifted their absorption band, and other types of N-dopant (e.g., pyridinic, pyrrolic, and amino centers) were found to have no significant impact on CQDs's absorption properties.

N-doping also benefits the fluorescence properties of CQDs to a certain extent. Zhang et al. [93] used three types of protein-rich bee pollen to synthesize N-CQD via the HT processes, and notably, there was a proportional relationship between the QY of three CQDs and the N content of the raw bee pollen, suggesting that more electrons were trapped by newly formed surface states introduced by N atoms. This was in agreement with findings by Liu et al. [94]. N-CQDs could also exhibit UCPL and excitation-independence fluorescence properties, which consequently promote photocatalytic applications. For instance, Heng et al. [95] documented the UCPL characteristics of N-CQDs using ethylenediamine. Xu et al. [96] prepared N-CQDs with urea as an N source and also observed UCPL properties. However, a detailed mechanistic explanation of how N-doping induces UCPL characteristics of CQDs is still missing. In a study by Liu et al. [94], excitation-independence behavior was observed in synthesized N-CQD and was attributed to the passivating role of urea as an N dopant. This finding aligns with the study by Xu et al. [90], in which the PL peak remained fixed at a wavelength of 453 nm when the excitation wavelengths changed from 290 nm to 900 nm (Fig. 6b). The author explained that the recombination process was dominated by a single near-band-egde transition mode, rather than by surface state emissions or defect-related emissions, suggesting that the surface of fabricated N-CQDs was highly passivated by N-doping.

In summary, due to the ease of CN bond formation, various N-dopants are commonly employed as passivating or functionalizing agents to modify the properties of CQDs. The effect of N-dopants on the absorption ability of CQDs varies depending on the types of dopants. N-doping can passivate surface states or defects in CQDs, contributing to excitation-independent behavior. N-doping has the potential to induce UPLC characteristics of CQDs, although the detailed mechanisms behind this remain not fully elucidated.

Similar to the effect of introducing new surface states in N-doping, sulfur (S) -doping provides CQDs with abundant high-energy active sites. However, there are limited studies to explore S-doing. In a study by Rani et al. [97], S-CQDs were prepared by mixing lignin extracted from empty fruit bunches with sodium thiosulfate in an HT reactor at 180 °C for 8 h. The surface of S-CQDs was observed to contain several polar functional groups, such as SH, CH, CO, CO, and OH (Fig. 7a), which contributed to the existence of active sites and the high solubility of the S-CQDs. As a result, the CQDs exhibited a super-high efficiency of 98 % in the photodegradation of crystal violet dye. This was in agreement with Wu et al. [98], who synthesized S-CQDs for highly accurate detection of Fe3+ ion. FITR analysis of these S-CQDs indicated the presence of CS bond. The plausible chemical structure of S-CQDs was proposed by Wang et al. [99] as shown in Fig. 7b, which involved the lattice substitution in S-doping processes.

S-doping also affects the QY of CQDs. Xu et al. [101] prepared S-CQDs with a remarkably improved QY of up to 67 %, using sodium citrate and sodium thiosulfate as precursors via HT processes. The author emphasized that an appropriate ratio between S and C is crucial for significantly increasing the QY of S-CQDs. However, they also noted that excessive S could block passivated surface defects, leading to a decrease in QY. This observation aligned with the study of Yang et al. [100], where sulfuric acid was used as the S-doping agent to synthesize CQDs at 200 °C for 4 h, resulting in an enhancement of QY from 1 % to 32 %. The authors also found that such synthesized S-CQDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior and UCPL property when excited from 670 to 770 nm (Fig. 7 cd), which was likely caused by the homogeneous surface structure and narrow size distribution of S-CQDs.

Phosphorus (P) doping also acts as a passivating agent, enhancing PL properties and photostability of CQDs. Chai et al. [102] synthesized P-CQDs from m-aminophenol using phosphoric acid as a phosphorus dopant via HT processes. The resulting P-CQDs contained rich functional groups, such as -COOH, -OH, PO, and demonstrated excitation-independent behavior from 300 nm to 460 nm. These contributed to their high photostability and environmental stability, making them as effective antibacterial agents against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. These observations conformed to the study of Zhou et al. [103], where the P-CQDs contained P of 8.5 %, O of 24.2 % and C of 65.4 %, forming sufficient oxygen-containing groups (e.g., PO and -OH bonds). These promoted electron generation, resulting in a QY of up to 25 % and excellent biolabeling ability.

In another study by Kalaiyarasan et al. [104], P-CQDs were synthesized using an aqueous mixture of trisodium citrate and phosphoric acid. This study revealed that the QY of P-CQDs was highly dependent on reaction temperature, reaction time, and doping ratio. The maximum QY of P-CQDs was achieved at 140 °C, 12 h, and a trisodium citrate to phosphoric acid ratio of 1:1. The author also found that excessive carbonization and oxidation reduced the absorption capacity and fluorescence stability of P-CQDs. The passivation effect of P-doping was observed, leading to monochromatic fluorescence (excitation-independent nature) (Fig. 8 ). The passivation role of P-doping was further supported by Preethi et al. [105], who synthesized P-CQDs with a high QY of 32.16 %, and applied them as fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ ion.

Several attempts have been made to dope Chlorine (Cl) atoms into CQDs to enhance the photocatalytic performances, leveraging its strong electronegativity to modify surface charge and induce additional electronic states. In a study by Murali et al. [106], Cl-doped CQDs were synthesized using sucralose as both the C and Cl source for methylene blue dye degradation. The as-prepared Cl-CQDs had a highly negative surface charge of −46.5 mV, compared to −17.1 mV for CQDs without doping. The authors explained that a large number of outermost electrons from the doped Cl atoms created an electron cloud around the Cl-CQDs, which combined with O elements to generate an internal electric field. This speculation was confirmed by bandgap determination, where the Cl-CQDs displayed a narrower bandgap of 3.24 eV compared to 4.07 eV for original CQDs. As a result, the Cl-CQDs showed a high photocatalytic efficiency in methylene blue photo-degradation, primarily due to the internal electric field, as presented in Fig. 9a. The structure of Cl-CQDs was presented in Fig. 9b. These results were in agreement to the study of Ansari et al. [82], who conducted a comparative study of Cl-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, FeCl-CQDs, all synthesized under same HT conditions (200 °C, 5 h). Cl-CQDs exhibited a bandgap reduction to 2.49 eV, compared to 2.96 eV for CQDs without doping and 2.90 eV for Fe-CQDs. These were supported by photocurrent results. The author emphasized that the co-existence of Cl and O elements automatically formed an internal electric field, enabling charge separation and quenching the charge recombination. To sum up, similar to other non-metal atom dopants, Cl doping can induce new surface states in CQDs and impact photocatalytic properties by generating more electrons, which work with O atoms to form an internal electric field.

Boron (B) doping differs from other non-metal atom because the formation of BC bonds results in electronic deficiency, leading to surface defects in B-CQDs. On the other hand, these surface defects can attract electrons from their environment or from neighboring atoms, potentially altering the fluorescence properties of CQDs. In a study by Shen et al. [107], B-CQDs were synthesized by mixing boric acid with ethylenediamine via HT processes at 260 °C for 12 h. The synthesized B-CQDs exhibited strong fluorescence under UV light, with a QY of up to 22 %. Hence, it became a promising candidate for applications as light emitting diode (LED) converter materials and cell-imaging agents. The electron-deficient nature of the B atom led to the formation of surface defects that attracted and localized additional electrons. These defects hindered charge transfer between electron donors and acceptors, thereby reducing non-radiative recombination and minimizing fluorescence quenching. Ma et al. [108] produced B-CQDs using three different types of B sources (sodium tetraphenylborate, borax, and boric acid) via HT processes. The B-CQDs doped with sodium tetraphenylborate achieved a QY of 42 %. In this study, B-CQDs were applied for ultrasensitive sensors for detecting catechol and glutathione. B atom content was found to be only 0.88 % but emitted a strong fluorescence. This is likely attributed to the abundance of hybridized surface chemical groups. In summary, the electronic deficiency of B atoms induces new surface defects in B-CQDs, promoting the QY and fluorescence property and thus making them highly suitable for applications in LED convertor, cell imaging, and sensors. Further studies should focus on exploring various B dopants to unlock the full potential of B-CQDs.

Despite single-atom doping brings many advantages to CQDs, its effectiveness is often limited by the difference in chemical structure between C and doping elements. Various studies therefore have explored co-doping two or more elements into CQDs expecting synergistic effects that further enhance their photochemical properties.

Compared to single-atom doping, co-doping CQDs provides greater benefits for photocatalytic activity. In a comparative study of [109], Cl-CQDs, Fe-CQDs, and FeCl-CQDs were synthesized under the same HT conditions (200 °C, 5 h), and applied to RhB photo-degradation. The FeCl-CQDs demonstrated a lower bandgap (2.15 eV) compared to Cl-CQDs and Fe-CQDs, which correlated with their higher photocatalytic efficiency for RhB degradation. Similarly, Yashwanth et al. [110] prepared NP-CQDs via HT processes and observed higher photocatalytic efficiency on MB degradation compared to N-CQDs, and P-CQDs. The calculated work functions were 4.32 eV, 3.07 eV, 3.01 eV, and 2.81 eV for CQDs, N-CQD, P-CQDs, and NP-CQDs, respectively. The author attributed this to additional energy states from N, P doping, which shifted the Fermi energy level closer to CB (Fig. 10 ab), hence promoting photocatalytic efficiency in MB dye degradation. That NP-CQDs also showed enhanced absorption of visible light.

In a N participated co-doped CQD, N atom plays a critical role in the fluorescence properties. Ye et al. [113] prepared NB-CQDs using different borate dopants (boric acid, manganese borate, and sodium tetraborate) under HT conditions. The author found that sodium tetraborate-doped CQDs that had a higher concentration of N, but lower concentration of B, exhibited a QY of up to 68.28 %, compared to 29.01 % for boric acid-doped CQDs, and 51.42 % for manganese borate-doped CQDs. This implied N atoms play a key role in the synergistic effect in co-doped CQDs. That agrees with the research conducted by Xu et al. [111], who prepared two types of NS-CQDs using different nitrogen sources (urea VS. DMF and urea) at the same HT conditions. The author found that DMF-participated CQDs with higher N content but lower S content, exhibited lower PL intensity compared to only urea-doped CQDs (Fig. 10 c), furtherly indicating the key role of Wang et al. [114] also synthesized N-CQDs, P-CQDs, and NP-CQDs, and found that P had a minor impact on the optical properties of N,P-CQDs. The enhancement in PL intensity primarily resulted from the interaction between ammonium groups (-NH4), epoxy groups, and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs.

Multiple atoms doping introduces more electron sites and surface defects than single or dual atoms doping, though it is not widely reported due to the complexity of synergistic processes and challenging synthesis conditions.

Triple-element doping can attach more functional groups to CQDs surface, increasing electron sites for photocatalysis. Wang et al. [115] doped N, S, and P into CQDs, resulting in NSP-CQDs with abundant O- and N- functional groups and small amount of P- and S- functional groups. These CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior and were highly effective as fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection. Li and Xu [116] synthesized NSP-CQDs using phosphoric acid solution (H3PO4) as P source and sulfonamide as S and N sources. That NSP-CQDs reached the highest QY of 33 %, and demonstrated sensitive fluorescence properties to pH and Cl−, hence were successfully applied for Cl− detection in sweat. Das and Mohapatra [112] synthesized NSB-CQDs with a high QY of 28 %. That synthesized NSB-CQDs, rich in functional groups such as CN, CN, CO, CO, and -NH (Fig. 10d), were applied for ultrasensitive glucosamine sensing and targeted imaging of liver cancer cells. The detailed synergistic mechanism among dopants deserves further exploration.

Overall, co-doping CQDs introduces more functional groups and surface energy levels and reduces bandgap of CQDs. Multiple atoms doping provides additional electron sites on the surface of CQDs, further enhancing their fluorescence properties. Despite advancements in dual and multiple atoms doped, particularly involving N atoms, co-doping with both non-metal and metal atoms is rare. While N atom plays a key role in dual-atom doping, the synergistic mechanisms between various dopants, especially in multiple-atom doping, require further investigation.

Beyond modifying CQDs themselves, integrating CQDs into traditional photocatalysts improves the light harvesting, charge transfer, and thus overall photocatalytic activity. Traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2, g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, ZnO, face challenges related to their limited absorption capacity of visible light, high charge recombination rate, and low light utilization efficiency [117]. The unique optical and electronic properties of CQDs have led to incorporation of both doped and non-doped CQDs into traditional photocatalysts. HT process offers a green and sustainable approach to integrating CQDs with traditional photocatalysts, either by dissolving or recrystallizing them. This section explores the enhanced catalytic performance of various CQDs decorated photocatalysts via HT processes.

CQDs typically exhibit broad absorption capacity in UV spectral range, with extension into visible and near-infrared light ranges (NIR). CQDs-decorated photocatalysts have demonstrated enhanced absorption, thus promoting solar energy utilization. Fig. 11a shows the UV–visible spectrum of CQDs/TiO2 synthesized through HT processes [118,119,51]. Compared to bare TiO2, CQDs/TiO2 had a significantly stronger absorption intensity and an extended absorption edge from 390 to 425 nm. These results were similar to the study by Wang et al. [120], who reported a redshift in the absorption band of CQDs/TiO2, attributing to the formation of C-O-Ti bonds between CQDs and TiO2. Liu et al. [121] also observed a profound redshift in the absorption edge of as-prepared CQDs/CdS in visible light region. Di et al. [122] noted a marked redshift in absorption band of CQDs/Bi2WO6, compared to Bi2WO6. Tang et al. [123] conformed the enhancement effect of CQDs in absorption capacity of g-C3N4.

Bandgap, a fundamental characteristic of a photocatalyst, directly determines their photocatalytic efficiency by governing light absorption ability and charge carrier generation. Reducing bandgap is beneficial for improving photocatalytic activities, and CQDs decoration has been proven to decrease bandgap [126,127]. Huang et al. [128] reported that the bandgap of TiO2 decreased from 3.21 eV to 3.15 eV after CQDs decoration. Gawal and Golder [124] synthesized CQDs/CdS via HT processes and found that as the CQDs loading amount increased from 0.25 % to 1 % (w/w), the bandgap of CQDs/CdS decreased from 2.37 eV to 2.13 eV, lower than the 2.42 eV for pure CdS (Fig. 11b). In a study by Zhao et al. [129], although CQDs decoration had a minor effect on absorption edge of g-C3N4, the bandgap of g-C3N4 decreased from 2.7 eV to 2.62 eV.

Up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) is one of the most attractive properties of CQDs, enabling longer wavelength photons such as absorption and conversion to shorter wavelength emissions such as visible or UV light. Several studies have leveraged the UCPL property of CQDs by decorating them with traditional photocatalysts to enhance the overall efficiency of these photocatalysts. Kong et al. [130] explored the synergistic interaction between CQDs and Bi2WO6 and observed that the UCPL of CQDs led to a redshift toward NIR light in the CQDs/ Bi2WO6 composite. Similarly, Feng et al. [125] demonstrated that CQDs with UCPL properties enhanced the capture of NIR light via up-conversion in the CQDs/CdS/g-C3N4 system (Fig. 11c). In another study, Huang et al. [131] decorated UCPL-enabled CQDs with CdIn2S4/CdS and found that the light energy emitted by CQDs through up-conversion was efficiently utilized by CdIn2S4/CdS.

Overall, CQDs decoration significantly enhances the performance of traditional photocatalysts by broadening their absorption range into the UV, visible, and near-infrared regions, thus improving solar energy utilization. CQDs not only increase absorption intensity and induce redshifts in the absorption edge of traditional photocatalysts but also effectively reduce their bandgap, leading to improved light absorption and enhanced generation of charge carriers. The UCPL property of CQDs plays a crucial role in converting longer-wavelength light into shorter-wavelength emissions, which further boosts the efficiency of photocatalyst composites. These enhancements underscore the importance of CQDs decoration in advancing the efficiency and functionality of photocatalytic systems.

The unique PL properties of CQDs influence PL behaviors of CQDs-decorated photocatalysts. PL intensity and PL lifetime are critical parameters that provide insights into charge carrier dynamics within CQDs-based photocatalysts. Specifically, PL intensity reflects recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs. A decrease in PL intensity, indicative of PL quenching, suggests efficient carrier separation and transfer [132]. The PL lifetime evaluates the average time that an excited electron remains in an excited state before returning to the ground state by emitting a photon [133]. A longer PL lifetime generally indicates slower recombination of e−-h+ pairs. Fig. 12c shows that the PL intensity of pure CdS is significantly higher compared to its composites with varying amounts of CQDs (0.25 to 1.00 wt%) [124]. This suggests that CQDs decoration enhances charge separation and reduce radiative recombination rate in the CdS composites. The author claimed that the excellent electron-conducting characteristics of the delocalized π-conjugated structure in CQDs promoted rapid electron transfer from the CB of CdS to CQDs. The direction of electron transfer from semiconductor to CQDs highlighted the role of CQDs as electron acceptors, which can effectively capture and store electrons from the semiconductor, preventing recombination with holes and enabling subsequent redox reactions. The average PL lifetime of CQDs/CdS was also found to be higher than that of CdS (3.74 ns vs. 1.59 ns respectively). However, when the CQDs loading amount exceeded 0.5 wt%, the PL intensity of CQDs/CdS began to increase (Fig. 12c), suggesting that over-loading CQDs can negatively affect PL properties [124], as also noted by Hong et al. [134].

TiO₂ generally exhibits low PL because it has an efficient charge separation and a high recombination resistance in its intrinsic state (Fig. 12a). Its wide bandgap and surface states favor non-radiative recombination, resulting in minimal light emission. PL intensities of CQDs/TiO₂ (101) and CQDs/TiO₂ (001) composites were significantly lower than CQDs themselves but higher than TiO₂ without decoration [120]. This indicates effective PL quenching occurrence due to the interaction between CQDs and TiO₂, enhancing charge transfer and reducing e−-h+ recombination on CQDs. The PL intensities of CQDs/TiO₂ (101) and CQDs/TiO₂ (001) composites were close, indicating that the specific crystal facets of TiO₂ (101) versus (001) do not have significant difference in PL behavior when combined with CQDs. The time-resolved PL decay kinetic curves (Fig. 12b) further revealed the PL dynamics of CQDs/TiO2, showing that the PL lifetime of CQDs/TiO2 was dramatically prolonged to over 35 ns with decreasing CQDs content. When incorporating N-CQDs to BiOBr, Yu et al. [135] reported that oxygen vacancies (OVs) were simultaneously induced on the BiOBr surface. The interaction facilitated more efficient electrons trapping and accelerated the separation of e−-h+ pairs, as indicated by a decrease in PL intensity of N-CQDs/OVs BiOBr composites (Fig. 12d). The energy disorder caused by the strong local charge density around OVs, has been identified as a key factor that disrupts the binding energy between electrons and holes in excitons, enabling to accelerate their separation efficiency in photocatalytic system [78,136].

Upon excitation by absorbed photons, electrons in photocatalysts are promoted to higher energy levels, generating e−-h+ pairs. The separation and movement of these charge carriers within photocatalyst give photocurrent [137]. Photocurrent density and charge resistance are two important parameters that reflect the efficiency of charge separation and transport processes in photocatalysts. In Fig. 13a, TiO2 decorated with 4 % CQDs showed the highest photocurrent density compared to the virgin TiO2 that exhibited no obvious photoelectric response under visible light [118,51]. These indicated that CQDs indeed contributed to the separation of photoinduced carriers and narrowed the bandgap of TiO2. A similar result was observed in the CQDs/CdS composite (Fig. 13b) [121]. All CQDs/CdS composites had much higher photocurrent than CdS. Furthermore, the onset potentials of all CQDs/CdS electrodes showed a negative shift compared to CdS (e.g., 1 % CQDs/CdS: −1.1 V vs. CdS: −0.5 V), suggesting that carrier transport was more efficient in CQDs/CdS. The CQDs/CdS composite also displayed much higher photosensitivity in photocurrent response during on-off illumination cycles. The photogenerated charge migration and separation efficiency of CQDs/g-C3N4, were also investigated under visible light (λ > 420 nm) (Fig. 13 cd) [138]. CQDs/g-C3N4 presented higher photocurrent density and lower charge resistance compared to bare g-C3N4, implying more efficient photogenerated e−-h+ pair separation rate and a longer electron lifetime.

Some ternary composites displayed further enhanced conductivity due to formation of special Z-scheme heterojunction. Feng et al. [125] reported that CdS/CQDs/g-C3N4 system showed the highest photocurrent intensity compared to g-C3N4, CdS/g-C3N4, and CQDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 13e). The authors attributed this to the formation of a Z-scheme heterojunction, where CQDs served as electrons mediators to accolade electron transfer at the CdS/CQDs/g-C3N4 interface (Fig. 13f). Notably, the ternary composite also exhibited a photocurrent signal under NIR light, which was due to the UCPL characteristics of CQDs. These results corresponded to Tian et al. [139] who prepared g-C3N4/CQDs/BiVO4 and observed significantly enhanced interfacial photocurrent intensity and minimized charge transfer resistance. The heterojunction formed between g-C3N4 and BiVO4 improved band alignment, promoting the charge transfer with CQDs serving as mediators.

Photostability refers to the ability of photocatalysts to maintain their structural integrity and photocatalytic efficiency over prolonged period under continuous light irradiation or specific environmental conditions. The chemical structure of CQDs is crucial to their photostability, which in turn affects the overall photostability of CQDs-based photocatalysts. Several studies have explored the photostability of CQDs decorated photocatalysts. In the study of Liu et al. [121], CQDs/CdS was tested for its stability in degradation of Rhodamine B dye over three cycles, showing sustained high degradation efficiency (Fig. 14a). Duo et al. [140] reported that CQDs/BiOBr showed only 9.5 % decline in degradation efficiency after seven circles, compared to a 46.4 % decline for BiOBr alone (Fig. 14b). This was attributed to UPCL properties of CQDs, which allowed conversion of visible light into 350–600 nm wavelength, refreshing OVs on BiOBr in situ and thereby maintaining stable photocatalytic activity. However, the photostability of CQDs decorated photocatalysts have been found to be sensitive to pH variations. Zhao et al. [118] prepared CQDs/TiO2, and applied it to photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI). The efficiency of photocatalytic reduction decreased with increasing pH (Fig. 14c), attributing to changes in isoelectric point of CQDs/TiO2, which altered the surface charge.

In more complex ternary composite systems, CQDs and traditional photocatalysts have illustrated synergistic interaction that further increased the overall photostability. He et al. [141] synthesized a three-dimensional network catalyst system of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, which maintained 91.5 % photodegradation efficiency after six cyclic experiments (Fig. 14d). The system formed a more complete electron flow pathway, where accumulated electrons flowed from Ag3PO4 to NP-CQDs and eventually to rGH, decreasing recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs. These observations were comparable to the study by Zhang et al. [142], who synthesized FeOOH QDs/CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunction that exhibited excellent stability in oxytetracycline degradation, maintaining 75.3 % efficiency after five runs.

With the advancement of artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML) has been demonstrated a transformative force in traditional chemical and materials science, enabling the mathematical and statistical translation of experimental data into accurate theoretical interpretation, relationship mapping, performance optimization, and even in guiding the design of synthetic strategies for chemical materials. The synthesis of CQDs, particularly via HT processes, remains largely trial-and-error based, making it time-consuming, lab-intensive, and costly. Research efforts have increasingly focused on leveraging ML and AI to optimize and rationalize CQDs synthesis (Table 2 ).

The most common application of ML in CQDs synthesis is the prediction and optimization of CQDs properties by correlating experimental parameters to performance outcomes. This process typically requires a large database, where multiple input features (e.g., reaction conditions) and a single response variable (e.g., QY) are used to train predictive models. Identifying the most influential input variables and understanding their impact on the response are critical for model construction.

Han et al. [143] successfully built up several regression-based ML models (XGBoost regressor, multilayer perceptron, support vector machine regressor, and Gaussian process regressor) using data from 391 HT CQDs synthesis experiments. Those models revealed the relationship between experimental conditions, including volume of alkaline catalyst (co-catalyst), reaction temperature, reaction time, feedstock ratio, and ramp rate, and the resulting QYs. Those models achieved a high QY of up to 39.9 % and identified the feedstock ratio and volume of alkaline catalyst as the most influential features in achieving high-QY CQDs. Similarly, Chen et al. [144] utilized 480 experiments of CQDs synthesis and six machine learning models to predict the relationship between the experimental conditions and QYs. They found that the synthesis parameters (reaction time, and reaction temperature) were more significant in predicting QYs than the properties of the carbon precursor.

The optical properties of CQDs, including color tuning and emission behavior, have also been used as responses in ML models. Senanayake et al. [146] employed a database comprising 407 samples and constructed a model of artificial neural networks (ANNs) to elucidate the correlation relationship between various input features (reaction temperature, reaction time, reaction method, solvent, pH, purification method, precursor composition) and CQDs emission characteristics. Differing from the previous studies, this model incorporated the purification method as one of the input features, which was identified as a significant factor influencing the emission properties of CQDs. Wang et al. [147] also developed a deep convolution neural network (DCNN) model to predict the fluorescence color of CQDs. The model was trained on 170 proto-typical studies, with input features including feedstock type, feedstock ratio, reaction solvent, reaction temperature, and reaction time. The model achieved a strong predictive performance, with a correlation coefficient of 0.82 for the training set, indicating its reliability and powerful potential.

While these traditional ML approaches show great promise in experimental optimization, they are often constrained by the need for large datasets and the manual selection of input features. To overcome these limitations, Guo et al. [148] proposed a ML-integrated (multi-objective optimization) MOO strategy for HT synthesis of CQDs. Unlike conventional models that rely on bulk data processing followed by correlation analysis and optimization, this approach employed an iterative design loop that integrates data construction, MOO formulation, model-based recommendation, and experimental validation. Remarkably, this study used only 63 experimental samples to successfully and quickly determine the optimal synthesis conditions for full-color CQDs with a QY of up to 60 %. Key input features identified included reaction temperature, reaction time, catalyst type, and volume/mass of the precursor.

Despite these promising developments, current applications of machine learning in the field of CQDs remain largely confined to optimizing synthesis parameters and predicting performance metrics such as quantum yield and emission properties. The application of ML has not yet been widely extended to more advanced areas such as doping strategies or the rational design of CQD-decorated photocatalysts, which are crucial for expanding CQDs' utility in energy conversion and environmental applications. Moreover, existing ML approaches are still limited by key challenges such as the requirement for large, high-quality datasets, subjective selection of input features, and a lack of standardized data reporting. Future efforts should focus on developing more efficient and generalizable ML frameworks, potentially integrated with experimental feedback loops or high-throughput screening, to facilitate the intelligent design and optimization of CQDs-based photocatalysts. Overcoming these challenges will be essential to fully realize the potential of ML in advancing CQDs from material discovery to application-specific functionality.

As design strategies for CQD-based photocatalysts continue to advance through both experimental and data-driven approaches, their practical applications, especially in photocatalytic hydrogen production, warrant detailed exploration. Photocatalytic water splitting, a green and sustainable method for H2 generation, has been regarded as a promising strategy to mitigate the energy crisis. CQDs-based photocatalysts demonstrate significant potential in water splitting due to their abundant active sites, broadened light absorption ability, and enhanced photogenerated charge transfer ability. This section assesses the H2 evolution performance of different CQDs-based photocatalysts based upon the H2 generation rate (Table 3 ), along with the discussion about the effects of key experimental factors, such as synthesis method, sacrificial agents, co-catalysts, and reaction conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of CQDs based binary photocatalytic system for H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: Several attempts have been made to explore incorporating CQDs into various photocatalytic systems to enhance the overall efficiency of H2 production. Commonly investigated photocatalysts include CdS [170], TiO₂ [128], ZnIn₂S₄ [169], g-C₃N₄ [172], and others. When CQDs are integrated into these mono-photocatalysts, they primarily function as electron acceptor and photo-sensitizer. Smrithi et al. [170] synthesized red beetroots-CQDs decorated CdS and applied them in photocatalytic H2 generation with assistance of 0.35 M Na2S and 0.25 M Na2SO3 as sacrificial agent. The highest H2 evolution rate of 1582 μmol g−1 h−1 was achieved with 6 wt% CQDs/CdS, compared to 849 μmol g−1 h−1 for pure CdS. The improved H2 generation rate of CQDs/CdS was attributed to the excellent conductivity of CQDs, the increased number of active sites provided by CQDs, and light absorption enhanced by CQDs. The electron transfer pathway was revealed to occur from CdS to CQDs, confirming the role of CQDs as electron acceptor. The reusability and photostability of CQDs/CdS were validated through five repeated cycles, showing consistent H₂ evolution in the four cycles. In addition, the XRD analysis was conducted after the recyclability test of CQDs/CdS (Fig. 15e), which showed the similar patterns and indicated that the excellent photo-corrosion resistance of CQDs/CdS.

A study conducted by Gogoi et al. [154] demonstrated the enhancement of photocatalytic H2 activity in CdS through the incorporation of orange peel-derived CQDs via chemical deposition method. The optimized composite, 0.4 wt% CQDs/CdS, showed the highest H2 generation rate (309,000 μmol g−1 h−1) and apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) (32.6 %) in the presence of sacrificial agents (Fig. 15a) among of all composites. It was nearly 1.5 times higher than that of pure CdS. Compared to pure CdS, the 0.4 wt% CQDs/CdS presented high stability over three cycles of photocatalytic experiments (Fig. 15f), highlighting the protective role of CQDs in photo-corrosion. Notably, the study also investigated the role of sacrificial agents in photocatalytic H2 processes. Under same experimental conditions, the absence of sacrificial agents resulted in a significant decline in H2 generation rate, with 0.4 wt% CQDs/CdS producing only 230 μmol g−1 h−1 of H2, while no H2 evolution was detected with CdS alone (Fig. 15b). These finding indicated the effectiveness of sacrificial agents, specifically 0.35 M Na2S and 0.25 M Na2SO3, in scavenging photo-generated holes, and reducing the recombination rate of e−-h+ pairs. The schematic pathway of H2 generation was presented in Fig. 15d, and a detailed bandgap structure was illustrated in Fig. 15c, in which the bandgap of CQDs/CdS was lower than CdS without CQDs.

The N-CQDs have been effectively incorporated into traditional photocatalysts for enhanced H2 production. For instance, Xu et al. [19] prepared NCQDs from urea-derived g-C3N4 and incorporated them with CdS via one-pot HT processes. The optimal composite, 33 wt% NCQDs/CdS, showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity, achieving a rate of 2306 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 7.3 time higher than that of pure CdS (316 μmol g−1 h−1). Interestingly, TEM images revealed that the 33 wt% NCQDs/CdS have concave holes in the surface (Fig. 16 ab), which implied the presence of surface defects generated by NCQDs. The author attributed the improved photocatalytic H2 performance to enhanced light reflection caused by these concave structures, which generated more photoelectrons. However, an excessive loading of NCQDs resulted in a decline in H2 production, implying the covering of the active sites and inhibition of light absorption by photocatalysts. Three-run cyclic stability tests and XRD analysis (Fig. 16 gh) results demonstrated that NCQDs reduced photo-corrosion compared to pure CdS. Wu et al. [164] also prepared N-CQDs/ZnS through HT processes. The 25 wt% N-CQDs/ZnS exhibited excellent H2 generation rate of 5700 μmol g−1 h−1, against with only 1110 μmol g−1 h−1 showed in utilization of 0.25 wt% CQDs/ZnS (Fig. 16f). The author attributed the enhanced performance to the high electron affinity of N atoms, which facilitated efficient electrons transfer from the CB of ZnS to N-CQDs, thereby enhancing H2 generation by increasing charge separation in ZnS. Additionally, a long-term catalytic stability test for 0.25 wt% CQDs/ZnS and pure ZnS under full-spectrum irradiation for 30 h was carried out and showed much better stability and photo corrosion resistance of CQDs/ZnS.

Interestingly, Duan et al. [161] reported an exceptionally high H2 generation rate of 19,800 μmol g−1 h−1 using N-CQDs/g-C3N4 (CNQDs/CNC), while simultaneously degrading pollutants (e.g., acridine orange, methyl orange, aniline, and phenol). The acridine orange and aniline functioned as both photosensitizers and sacrificial agents, significantly enhancing photocatalytic H2 evolution from 8800 μmol g−1 h−1 to 19,800 μmol g−1 h−1 and to 11,700 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively (Fig. 16 cd). The observed improvement was attributed to the fact that acridine orange and aniline were easily oxidized by photogenerated holes, thereby inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs. The detailed mechanism of photocatalytic H2 generation utilizing CNQDs/CNC was also proposed by the author (Fig. 16e). The CNQDs containing abundant of hydrophilic groups (-OH and -SO3H), formed a hydrogen bond network with interface water molecules. This hydrogen bond network lowered the energy barrier for proton transfer and facilitated the formation of a chain structure for proton transport between water molecules. In this special structure, protons quickly moved between water molecules and reached CNQDs, where they were trapped by CNQDs, then reduced to hydrogen atoms, ultimately releasing as H2.

Carbonylation of CQDs can also enhances photocatalytic systems in H2 generation. Zhou et al. [24] designed electron-withdrawing carbonylated CQDs through surface charge state modulation and incorporated them in CNN. The H2 generation rate of CQDs/CNN reached 54.50 mmol g−1 under 3.5 h of PL illumination (approximately 15,571 μmol g−1 h−1), maintaining the stability even after four cycles. This performance far exceeded that of CNN (7.59 mmol g−1) and surpassed nearly all binary CQDs-based photocatalysts, as compared in Table 3. The author explained that the carbonylated CQDs increased the electron-withdrawing sites in the CQDs/CNN photocatalytic system, serving as electron acceptors to efficiently promote the separation of e−-h+ pairs. The four-cycle photocatalytic stability tests were also conducted and proved the high stability and photo corrosion resistance of CQDs/CNN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of CQDs based ternary photocatalytic system for H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs-based ternary photocatalytic systems have attracted increasing attention due to their ability to form wherein specialized heterostructures, often involving Z scheme, traditional type II or S scheme, between CQDs and two photocatalysts. Z scheme heterostructures are the most widely studied, where CQDs act as mediator to facilitate electron transfer between semiconductors, suppress e−-h+ pairs recombination, thereby promoting H2 generation efficiency. Liu et al. [15] synthesized a solid-state Z-scheme Cd0.5Zn0.5S/CQDs/TiO2 film via HT carbonization and an in-situ reflux reaction. Such prepared ternary composites exhibited a super high H2 generation rate of 38,740 μmol g−1 h−1, which was nearly 3.4 times greater than that of TiO2 alone. The author elucidated that CQDs changed the direction of electrons transportation. Typically, electrons would transfer from the CB of TiO2 to that of Cd0.5Zn0.5S, while holes moved from the VB of TiO2 to the VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S. However, CQDs with excellent conductivity altered this pathway. In this case, electrons from the CB of TiO2 rapidly migrated to the VB of Cd0.5Zn0.5S (Fig. 17a), maintaining the redox capability of TiO2 while significantly extending the lifetime of charge carriers. Therefore, an excellent photocatalytic performance in water splitting was achieved. Also, after a 4- recycle experiment (2.5 h for each), there was no obvious decrease in H2 evolution efficiency, confirming the stability of this photocatalytic system. In a study of Chen et al. [20], synthesized CQDs/g-C3N4/InVO4 were effective for both the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B and H2 production. The 2 wt% CQDs/g-C3N4/InVO4 exhibited an H2 evolution rate of 2169 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 1.6 times higher than that of g-C3N4/InVO4. The author proposed a Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism wherein photogenerated electrons in the CB of InVO4 migrated and recombined with holes in the VB of g-C3N4 via the CQDs mediator (Fig. 17b). As a result, electrons were retained in the CB of g-C3N4, where they could effectively reduce H+ to generate H2.

The direction of electron transfer within CQDs-based photocatalytic system can vary depending on the synthesized heterostructure. Manchala et al. [152] prepared a traditional type-II heterostructure, LaFeO3/CdS/CQDs, through HT processes. The synthesized photocatalyst demonstrated a H2 generation rate of 25,302 μmol g−1 h−1 under solar light with lactic acid aqueous as the sacrificial agent, whose performance surpassed that of individual LaFeO3 (42 μmol g−1 h−1) and CdS (9604 μmol g−1 h−1), or binary LaFeO3/CQDs (849 μmol g−1 h−1), CdS/CQDs (12,775 μmol g−1 h−1), and LaFeO3/CdS (22,537 μmol g−1 h−1). Four repeated experiments under different irradiation times (1, 2, 3, and 4 h), confirming the stability and reproducibility of the LaFeO3/CdS/CQDs. The author proposed that photocatalytic system followed a traditional type-II electron transfer mechanism. In this pathway, photogenerated electrons transferred from the CB of CdS to the CB of LaFeO₃, while holes transferred from VB of LaFeO₃ to the VB of CdS, with CQD facilitating interfacial electron transfer (Fig. 18a). This mechanism ensured more electrons were preserved for H2 generation, maximizing H2 generation.

Xu et al. [166] synthesized an S-scheme heterostructure between CoMoO4, g-C3N4 and coal-based CQDs, achieving a H2 generation rate of 4916.63 μmol g−1 h−1 H2 generation rate at optimal pH of 9.
