The authors explained that the composite exhibited band bending due to the overlap of energy band structures of the two n-type semiconductors, resulting in an S type heterostructure. In this system, electrons spontaneously transferred from CB of g-C3N4 to VB of CoMoO4, generating an internal electric field within the composite. Differ from Z-scheme, the CQDs mainly increased charge density between CoMoO4 and g-C3N4, promoting the reduction reaction in the CB of CoMoO4 (Fig. 18b), which facilitated efficient electron transfer. Four consecutive H2 evolution cycle testes and XRD results of CQDs/CoMoO4/g-C3N4 before and after the reaction (Fig. 17 cd) revealed the good stability and reusability of the as-prepared photocatalyst. In another study, Xu et al. [18] reported the formation of an S-scheme heterostructure between ZnIn2S4, g-C3N4, and CQDs, which reached a high H2 generation rate of 17,580 μmol g−1 h−1. This performance was 10 times higher that pure g-C3N4 and 2 times higher than pure ZnIn2S4. The author emphasized the formation of band bending and internal electric field in the composite, but CQDs were regarded as electrons mediator to accelerate the interfacial electron transfer (Fig. 18c). Notably, the photocatalytic H2 application of this composite was extended to seawater splitting, achieving excellent photocatalytic activity with an H2 generation rate of around 12,500 μmol g−1 h−1.

Overall, in CQDs-based binary and ternary photocatalytic systems, the efficiency of H₂ evolution largely depends on the role of CQDs as photosensitizers, electron acceptors, and mediators, enhancing light absorption, providing active sites, and facilitating electron transfer between semiconductors. This helps prolong the lifetime of charge carriers and supports the reduction reaction at the conduction band, while sacrificial agents prevent recombination by scavenging holes. Although significant progress has been made, more research is needed to explore the dual functionality of CQDs in pollutant degradation and H₂ generation, and to better understand electron transfer mechanisms in different heterostructure schemes. The explored various schemes design involving Z scheme, S-scheme, and traditional type II scheme shown to improve CQDs-based systems, more scheme designs might offer promising potential for enhancing CQDs-based photocatalysts in H₂ evolution.
Despite abundant advantages and advancements of CQDs-based photocatalysts, several controversies, unsolved challenges remain unaddressed in the field of CQDs-based photocatalysis, as outlined below.

Several fundamental aspects remain controversial, particularly concerning their PL mechanism. Two dominant but competing theories have emerged, each supported by experimental evidence, yet neither is fully conclusive.

One school of thought attributes the fluorescence of CQDs to the quantum confinement effect, analogous to traditional semiconductor quantum dots. In this model, the size of the CQDs governs the bandgap energy; smaller particles possess wider bandgaps and emit light at shorter wavelengths [173]. In contrast, another widely accepted model emphasizes the role of surface and edge states, suggesting that the PL arises from localized energy levels introduced by functional groups, oxygen/nitrogen-containing moieties, and defects on the CQD surface [174]. These surface states can trap photoexcited electrons or holes, leading to radiative recombination independent of the core size.

Some researchers argue for a hybrid model, in which both quantum confinement and surface states contribute to the PL behavior, with their relative influence determined by factors such as particle size, degree of graphitization, heteroatom doping, and surface passivation. Zhu et al. [38] emphasized the synergistic effect of the quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π domain and the surface states of CQDs on their PL properties. Liu et al. [33] also pointed out that the quantum confinement effects coarsely adjusted the PL of CQDs, and the surface trap states finely adjusted PL of CQDs.

To resolve this ongoing debate, future studies should employ systematic experimental designs that decouple the effects of particle size and surface chemistry. Several advanced characterization techniques may offer deeper insights. For examples, time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) can help distinguish between core-state and surface-state emission based on differing lifetimes, single-particle fluorescence microscopy can reveal heterogeneity in emission characteristics, supporting the coexistence of multiple emission centers, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) can be used to correlate specific functional groups with emission peaks, and controlled doping and passivation studies can help determine how surface chemistry modulation impacts PL behavior. A combination of these techniques, applied to CQDs synthesized under varied HT conditions, would help clarify the dominant emission pathways and guide the rational design of CQDs for improved photocatalytic performance.

While citric acid has been a popular precursor for synthesizing CQDs, other organic acids have been largely underexplored. Citric acid is known for being a reliable and straightforward carbon source for producing CQDs, but it limits the diversity of CQD properties. The use of amino acids, such as glycine, alanine, and lysine, might present an opportunity to create N-doped CQDs. N doping can enhance the electronic properties and improve the photocatalytic performance of CQDs. These amino acids contain both carbon and nitrogen, making them ideal candidates for CQD synthesis, potentially leading to CQDs with enhanced PL, increased QYs, and better catalytic efficiency. A more extensive exploration of such alternative organic acids as CQD precursors is necessary to unlock new functional properties and further improve CQD-based photocatalysis.

CQDs derived from biomass represent an eco-friendly and sustainable alternative to those synthesized from conventional carbon sources. However, the interactions between individual components of biomass (e.g., lignin, cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, lipids) during CQD synthesis are still unclear. These components can influence key properties of the CQDs, such as size, surface chemistry, and PL characteristics. Therefore, mechanistic studies using single model components of biomass are essential to better understand their roles and interactions during synthesis, enabling more controlled and tunable CQDs. Advanced statistical methods, such as mixture design, are recommended to explore the significance of interactions between biomass components. Additionally, exploring CQDs derived from waste biomass remains an underdeveloped area with great potential. Waste-derived CQDs promote a circular economy and reduce environmental impact, but challenges such as introducing impurities, purification, and reproducibility must be addressed. Future research should prioritize developing scalable, cost-effective methods for synthesizing high-performance CQDs from biomass waste, while optimizing QY and photostability.

Doping CQDs, whether with metal or non-metal atoms, introduces new surface states and electronic properties that are critical for optimizing photocatalytic performance. The interaction of dopants with the CQD surface is complex and significantly impacts key properties such as charge separation, optical absorption, and catalytic activity. However, achieving uniformity in doping, precise control over dopant incorporation, and maintaining dopant stability remain major challenges. To address this issue, a precise control over precursor composition, pH adjustment, and the use of chelating agents can effectively stabilize dopant coordination during a synthesis process. Additionally, establishing a cyclic monitoring route that combines AI/ML technology within situ characterization techniques, such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), may offer significant potential for monitoring doping distribution and guiding synthesis adjustments.

For the selection of dopants, non-metal doping, particularly with elements such as N, S, P, Cl, and B, has received considerable attention. N doping enhances the QYs and reduces e−-recombination, making it one of the most studied dopants [109]. Other dopants, such as Cl and B, have unique potential. However, research into these dopants remains limited, and systematic studies are required to fully understand their potential. Metal doping also offers the opportunity to create localized energy levels within the CQD bandgap, facilitating enhanced charge separation and preventing e−-h+ recombination [77]. Despite its potential, metal doping is underexplored due to concerns over deep defect, toxicity and environmental risks. Furthermore, the long-term stability of metal-doped CQDs, including the risk of metal leaching, has not been sufficiently investigated. Co-doping with metal and non-metal elements holds promise for achieving synergistic effects that could further enhance CQD performance, but this approach requires precise control over dopant interactions and surface defect formation.

Doping CQDs, whether with metal or non-metal atoms, introduces new surface defects or states into the CQD structure. The challenge lies in managing and optimizing the induction of surface defects to enhance photocatalysis. While some defects can be beneficia, excessive deep defects can have a detrimental effect. Thus, more research is needed to develop strategies that carefully control the introduction of surface defects during the doping process. This may involve fine-tuning the dopant concentration, annealing conditions, or surface passivation techniques to maximize the beneficial shallow defects while minimizing harmful deep defects.

Traditional photocatalysts like TiO₂, and g-C₃N₄, exhibit limited absorption in the visible and NIR regions of the solar spectrum. While CQDs have a broader absorption range, extending from UV to visible and NIR, the challenge lies in optimizing this absorption for efficient solar energy utilization. Functionalizing CQDs with light-harvesting molecules like organic dyes or metal-organic frameworks (MOF) could further enhance visible and NIR light absorption [175], ensuring more efficient light utilization in photocatalytic processes. While CQDs enhance charge separation, the introduction of too many surface defects or the overloading of CQDs may lead to undesired recombination pathways. Ternary composites that utilize UCPL-enabled CQDs (e.g., CQDs/CdS/g-C₃N₄) have shown enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible and NIR light [125]. Future research should focus on exploring the combination of UCPL-active CQDs with a wider variety of photocatalysts to exploit the full solar spectrum for both H2 generation and environmental applications.

The photostability of CQDs-integrated photocatalysts is critical for their practical application, especially under prolonged light irradiation or varying environmental conditions. CQDs-integrated photocatalysts have shown to degrade in performance under certain conditions, such as high pH environments or continuous exposure to light [121,118,119,51]. One approach to enhancing the photostability of CQDs is to encapsulate them within silica shells, polymers, or other stable materials, which can protect the CQDs from environmental degradation while still allowing efficient light absorption and charge transfer. While CQDs provide excellent photocatalytic performance enhancements, the challenge of scaling up the production of CQDs-decorated photocatalysts remains. Scaling up HT processes by optimizing reactor designs and using renewable feedstocks can help achieve large-scale production of CQDs.

There is a notable lack of detailed mechanistic explanations and comparative studies between S- and Z-schemes in the context of CQDs-based photocatalysts. While both the S-scheme and Z-scheme have been explored for traditional semiconductors, their integration with CQDs is still in the early stages. More research is needed to understand how CQDs influence the charge transfer dynamics in these systems and to determine which scheme is more efficient for specific applications like H2 production or pollutant degradation. Comparative studies are crucial for determining the optimal photocatalytic schemes for different reactions. For example, in the Z-scheme, the ability to maintain high redox potential while facilitating charge separation is an advantage for H2 evolution, but this may come at the cost of lower light absorption. In contrast, the S-scheme may offer superior charge separation but could potentially reduce the redox potential. Evaluating the performance of CQDs in these schemes under different conditions (e.g., light intensity, pollutant concentration, or pH) is essential for optimizing their use in real-world applications. Additionally, understanding the long-term stability of these systems is crucial for developing photocatalysts that can operate efficiently over extended periods without degradation.

One of the most exciting possibilities for CQDs-based photocatalysts is their ability to perform dual functions, such as pollution degradation and photocatalytic H₂ evolution. CQDs have shown promise in environmental remediation, where they degrade organic pollutants in water [109]. Concurrently, CQDs can drive the water-splitting reaction that produces H₂ gas. Despite these dual capabilities, further exploration is required to fully realize the integration of these two processes within a single system. Achieving this dual functionality involves optimizing several factors, including light absorption properties, surface activity, and charge separation efficiency. For example, for pollutant degradation, CQDs must have a high surface area and the ability to generate reactive oxygen species, while for H₂ evolution, they need efficient electron transfer to facilitate H2 production. One of the main challenges in designing a multifunctional CQD-based photocatalyst is to balance the two processes. Each process may require different light intensities, catalytic conditions, or surface properties. Achieving optimal performance in both processes simultaneously without sacrificing the efficiency of either is a key challenge that requires further investigation. Exploring the synergy between pollutant degradation and H2 generation could lead to more efficient systems that address both energy production and environmental cleanup in a single step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Leveraging artificial intelligence and machine learning for advancing CQDs-based photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: To address these challenges, AI and ML hold the potential to revolutionize research and development in CQDs-based photocatalysts. For CQD synthesis, ML models can analyze extensive datasets to uncover correlations between precursor types, synthesis conditions, and PL behaviors, thereby enhancing our understanding of quantum size confinement and surface effects while optimizing CQD quality. In the CQD doping, AI can predict optimal dopant types, concentrations, and configurations to improve charge separation and optical characteristics, while minimizing the formation of undesirable deep defects. Additionally, ML can simulate dopant interactions with CQD surfaces, enabling precise control over doping uniformity and stability. For CQDs-integrated photocatalysts, ML can aid in optimizing heterostructure designs by identifying the ideal material combinations to maximize light absorption and charge separation across the solar spectrum. Furthermore, AI can support the development of scalable and eco-friendly synthesis approaches by optimizing reactor designs and processing parameters. By integrating these tools with advanced characterization techniques, such as in-situ spectroscopy, AI and ML can provide real-time insights and feedback, enabling the continuous refinement of CQDs-based photocatalysts.

This review consolidates key advancements in the synthesis, modification, and application of CQDs-based photocatalysts for H2 production. It provides comprehensive evaluations and valuable insights into the design and optimization of CQDs-based photocatalysts and highlights the existing challenges and outlooks for CQDs doping, decoration and photocatalytic H2 production.

A variety of feedstocks, including chemicals and biomass, have been successfully utilized to synthesize CQDs. The role of metal and non-metal doping in enhancing the properties of CQDs, especially tuning their optical characteristics and improving charge transfer efficiency, has been highlighted. Doping demonstrates strong potential for the precise modulation of photocatalytic performance. CQDs also play a crucial role in boosting the overall efficiency of CQDs-decorated photocatalysts through enhancing light absorption, facilitating charge separation, and improving electron transfer. As for the CQDs-based photocatalytic H2 production, CQDs can enhance the photocatalytic performance by acting as photosensitizers and electron acceptors within binary systems. In ternary systems, CQDs function as electron mediators, prolonging charge carrier lifetimes, with their effectiveness highly linked to heterostructure design. Despite considerable progress in CQDs-based photocatalysts, several challenges remain. A deep understanding of CQDs-dopant interactions is essential for optimizing photocatalytic performance. Additionally, exploring CQDs in emerging photocatalytic schemes, such as S-scheme systems, offers a promising direction for future research.

Future advancements in this field could be significantly accelerated by leveraging AI and ML tools. This review also highlights the emerging role of data-driven approaches in CQDs research, particularly in accelerating synthesis processes and reducing dependence on conventional trial-and-error methodologies. Despite their potential, current applications of AI/ML remain limited, especially in dopant optimization and heterostructure configuration. Moving forward, AI-guided strategies could facilitate the rational design of high-performance CQD-based photocatalysts by identifying optimal dopant combinations and heterojunction architectures. ML models are capable of revealing correlations between structural features and photocatalytic performance, thereby guiding targeted improvements. When combined with advanced characterization techniques, these data-driven approaches offer a promising pathway to fully unlock the potential of CQD-based systems for next-generation hydrogen production.

Zijing Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rahil Changotra: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis. Mita Dasog: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Gurpreet Singh Selopal: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Jie Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Quan Sophia He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
